title: Born on the Cusp of Brown
summary: Interviewed by Willie Griffin of the Southern Oral History Program in Albany, Georgia, in March 2013, Sam Young, Jr. recounts being born in Worth County, Sylvester, Georgia, on March 13, 1950, just as Brown v. Board of Education loomed, and a father of the same name who was active in local politics and kept the family abreast of the issues by making them watch the news every day.

title: Freedom of Choice and the School Bus
summary: Young describes how Freedom of Choice let Black students stay in the all-Black school or transfer to the white one, his decision to transfer, walking to school past churches while white children on the buses hung out the windows yelling racial names, and the children throwing rocks at the bus and slipping through the cleared turpentine paths in the woods.

title: Held Back Because I Could Not Read
summary: He tells how his father kept him back a grade because he could not read, putting him in school alongside a sister so people thought they were twins, recalls strict teachers with no cheating and the time he was caught sitting on a spelling book and spanked, and the lesson his parents drilled in: never follow the crowd, you are born an individual and you die an individual.

title: The Devil's Den and the Detention Center
summary: Young recounts vandals painting his Worth County Baptist church and writing that it was the devil's den, a local who was made to repaint the whole thing, and two of his cousins, around fourteen, arrested off the school bus after one beat a white boy and taken to the Youth Detention Center in Albany, where the sheriff hid their location for three days until attorney C.B. King threatened to have him arrested.

title: Shot for Refusing to Say Mister
summary: He describes being in the presence of shootings, including a deacon and Sunday school man whose teenage employer's son demanded to be called Mister, and when the man refused, the boy's father came out with a shotgun and shot him, the buckshot lodging in his face and hands that he had thrown up to shield his head, and names the shooter as Albert Gill and the wounded man as C.T. Rockwell.

title: No Land to Pass On
topic: Foreclosures and a Line of Credit
summary: Young reflects on the racial pressure aimed at his father, the foreclosures that took Black land, and a local white grocer and hardware owner who extended his father a line of credit to farm, lamenting that unlike white families whose land passed down through generations, there was no such inheritance for Black people until recent ASCS, later Farm Service Agency, programs.

title: Ten Sisters and Seven Brothers
summary: He lists the cotton, corn, peanuts, and vegetables he grew up planting, explains that the large family of ten sisters and seven brothers existed in part to gather the crops, and recalls being the oldest son with two older sisters and graduating from Worth County High School in Sylvester in 1968, where he believed he was the only male in a class of four or five young women.

title: Firestone and a Need for Education
summary: Young recounts his first public job at Firestone after high school, running into tough civil rights problems and being let go, the brutal labor of busting concrete in deep foundations that convinced him he needed more education, and learning of the Southwest Georgia Project for Community Education through two older sisters who already worked there.

title: The Mentors Who Took Me In
summary: He describes working with the Southwest Georgia Project until 1983 and attending Albany Technical College for graphic art to help start a newspaper, explains the Project as a community education group covering the region with a scholarship program, and credits the mentors who took him in and taught him politics, Charles Sherrod, Randolph Battle, and Robert Mants, along with an attorney named Mary Young who worked alongside C.B. King.

part: New Communities and the Land Suit
title: A Huge Tract for Self-Sufficiency
summary: Young explains New Communities Inc as the land arm of the Southwest Georgia Project, how Charles Sherrod learned in New York of an unposted tract for sale in Southwest Georgia and pursued the idea of buying it to build a self-sufficient Black community, the land secured around 1968, the youth who came from different states to learn farming, and the peanut allotment of more than three hundred acres.

part: New Communities and the Land Suit
title: Years and Years Before the Suit Paid
summary: He recounts the class action suit in which the federal government finally admitted discrimination against Black farmers by the ASCS, the long wait from denials in the late 1980s and mid 1990s until money came through in 2009 when many had given up, the farm being disqualified because of the Project's other activities, and Shirley and Charles Sherrod later buying a new plot of land in the area.

title: Denied a Phone, Denied a Future
topic: Living on the Farm, Then Letting Go
summary: Young describes the dwellings on the farm where he moved his wife and children after marrying in 1974, the local families and project people who lived there too, and how they were denied phones and never taken kindly by locals and higher-ups when Black people gained control of something better, and reflects on years of disappointment until reconnecting by chance with Ms. Sherrod inspired him to get back involved for the sake of Black youth.

title: Address the Issue Then
summary: He affirms that the Southwest Georgia Project never died out and was always active even as he went his own separate way, and offers his words of wisdom: that you have to stand up and address an issue when it arises rather than wait, so that even if it does not go your way you will have made your discontent known.

title: Synectics and Seeing the Country
summary: Young recalls how the Project let him travel to New York City and the Carolinas and see far more than other young people his age, a New York seminar called Synectics that taught group problem solving on camera, the first time he saw himself recorded, and how he brought those skills back to his group, closing with gratitude for the chance to share his story.
